schools.summary: Ellicott names Nahash and guesses the old kindness; K&D frame the chapter as the last bright page before David's fall.
Henry: gratitude that does not weigh the grounds of a kindness resolves to repay it; comfort to children and mourners.
Gill dates the notice of Ammonite spoils in ch. 8:12 as written by anticipation of this very war.
Wesley notes the standing friendship; Clarke conjectures Nahash was Saul's old foe who befriended David as Saul's rival.
Gill reads the embassy as a friendly compliment of condolence on the father's death.
K&D render it 'to comfort by the hand of his servants,' already setting the trap the princes will spring.
Ellicott gives the strategic fear over fortified Rabbah; K&D read the loaded grammar of the two questions.
Clarke: courts breed insincerity, and courtiers conscious of their own falseness suspect it in others.
Gill identifies the speakers as the nobles and ministers about the young king.
Henry: unfounded suspicion argues a wicked mind; the well-meant is ill-interpreted by men who love only themselves.
K&D and Ellicott cite Arvieux and Niebuhr on the beard as the extremest Eastern insult; David never the aggressor.
Clarke: the beard sworn by and pledged, cut only in mourning or slavery; Wesley: the severed garment bared their nakedness.
Gill: they were ambassadors under the protection all nations owe an envoy, come unarmed on an errand of comfort.

themes: violation of the law of nations

schools.summary: K&D: David spared his own eyes the insult; Ellicott on Jericho's curse and continuance; Clarke on its private cottages.
Henry: unjust reproaches wear off and shame their authors, as the beards grew again; wait patiently for God (Ps. 37:6).

outliers.note: Adds the probing afterthought that David was well served for courting a pagan prince Israel might not admit to the congregation to the tenth generation (Deut. 23:3).

schools.summary: K&D and Ellicott tally the hired thousands and the talent price; the Hebrew is 'made themselves stink,' mustering at Medeba.
Clarke maps Beth-rehob, Zobah, Maacah, and Ish-tob, the land where Jephthah once took refuge.
Henry: had they humbled themselves a token satisfaction might have atoned; the arming is impenitence courting ruin.
Gill: knowing David incensed they neither appeased him nor made satisfaction but bought swords.
K&D: 'the mighty men' stands in apposition to 'all the host' — the whole battle-hardened army, not a select corps.
Clarke: David sent his worthies and the flower of his army, meeting the coalition with full strength.
Henry: a good prince carries war from his people's homes; those at war with the Son of David begin the war, and none prosper against God.
Gill: David heard of the Ammonite preparation and did not wait to be attacked at home.

themes: carrying the war to the enemy
war against the Son of David

schools.summary: K&D place the Syrians alone on the table-land by Medeba; Ellicott: two separate armies, chariots given open ground; Clarke fixes the gate as Medeba's.
Gill weighs whether the Ammonites came from Rabbah or Medeba and settles on the border city.
K&D: Joab chose the picked men against Aram; Ellicott: he threw his force between the two divisions, cutting the enemy in two while Israel stayed one body.
Gill renders the peril literally — the face of the battle before and behind.
Clarke reconstructs how the second army issued from the city to take Joab in the rear, forcing the split.
Gill: Abishai, a valiant commander under Joab, takes a wing so that Israel meets two armies with two.
K&D join the verse to the coming speech as the setting for Joab's charge to his brother.
Gill: the wings must have agreed on a signal for relief; neither brother grasps the glory, each runs to the other's danger.
Henry: mutual helpfulness is brotherly duty — Christ's soldiers strengthen one another as members of one body (1 Cor. 12:21).
Ellicott and K&D render 'Be strong, and let us strengthen ourselves,' and explain 'the cities of our God' — the land as Jehovah's own possession.
Clarke: a very fine military address — pro aris et focis, for God, families, and country.
Gill: Joab spoke in the hearing of the daunted people to hearten them before the charge.
Henry: when we have done our duty we leave the issue with God — valour and resignation held together.

themes: do your duty, leave the issue

schools.summary: Ellicott: the full weight thrown against the stronger Syrian division while Abishai held Ammon — tactics completely successful; K&D state the bare rout.
Gill: Joab struck the hired troops first, knowing their flight would dishearten the Ammonites who leaned on them.
Henry: Joab provided for the worst yet proved too strong for both — we do not hinder success by preparing for disappointment.
K&D and Ellicott narrate Ammon's retreat within the city and Joab's return; the incomplete victory gives the allies time to rally.
Gill: it was the flight of the trusted mercenaries that unnerved the Ammonites, who hardly fought.
Clarke: the Syrians fled to their own confines, the Ammonites into their city.

debates.question: Why was the victory over Ammon not at once pressed into a siege of the city?

sides.position: the season was already too late in the year, the siege of Rabbah belonging to the following spring (ch. 11:1)
uncertain — perhaps exhaustion, want of a siege-train, or the advanced season; the delay let the allies rally

schools.summary: Gill: the Syrians turned the defeat over among themselves, dreading its consequences and resolving to try again.
Henry: the baffled cause makes head while any life remains; they knew not the thoughts of the Lord, gathering them as sheaves for judgment (Mic. 4:12).

themes: rallying against the Son of David

schools.summary: K&D bring the host to Helam under Shobach; Ellicott reads Hadarezer's supreme rally from beyond the Euphrates as the measure of the first defeat.
Clarke: 'the river' is the Euphrates; Hadarezer is the Hadadezer of ch. 8:3, resh and daleth confused.
Gill: this Hadarezer headed the whole Syrian confederacy, the other kings his servants.
Clarke: David gathered all Israel because so great a war required his own presence.
Gill: told of the Syrians' preparations, David did not wait but crossed Jordan to meet them.
Ellicott: David led the north in person while Joab probably held the Ammonites south, keeping the two foes apart.
K&D: Shobach mortally wounded; Ellicott: a crushing blow Syria never recovered, the smaller 'seven hundred' the truer figure, each text naming one arm.
Clarke: the numeral-letter for 7,000 and 700 were confused, accounting for the divergence from 1 Chron. 19:18.
Gill: the flight came only after an obstinate and bloody fight.
Wesley: the promise to Abraham and Joshua of borders to the Euphrates fulfilled; Clarke crowns David's eight wars, then warns of the fall to come (1 Cor. 10:12).
Ellicott: the vassal kings turned tributary, and it is probable Hadarezer too owned David's suzerainty (1 Kings 4:21).
Gill: the kings subject to Hadarezer transfer their allegiance to Israel.

themes: the shadow of the coming fall
